signature: Certificates for products of matrices under language constraints: automaton-indexed Lyapunov/multinorm certificates (path-complete graphs), constrained joint spectral radius, instability certificates via occupation-measure duality, marginal-case (rho=1) boundedness, and the decidability/undecidability boundary for 2x2 matrix semigroup problems.

finds.ref: A. A. Ahmadi, R. M. Jungers, P. A. Parrilo, M. Roozbehani, "Joint Spectral Radius and Path-Complete Graph Lyapunov Functions", SIAM J. Control and Optimization 52(1):687-717, 2014
finds.the_theorem: Thm 2.4: Let A={A_1..A_m} be a finite set of real nxn matrices and G(N,E) a PATH-COMPLETE graph (every finite word over A appears as the label sequence of some directed path) with edges labeled by words of the gamma-scaled set. If there exist positive, continuous, homogeneous functions V_i, one per node, with V_j(L((i,j))x) <= V_i(x) for all edges and all x, then JSR(A) <= 1/gamma. Quadratic V_i make this an SDP. Hierarchies (De Bruijn / path-dependent graphs of memory M) are asymptotically tight with quality factor n^{1/(2(M+1))}.
finds.the_match: Collatz branches homogenize to A_0=[[1/2,0],[0,1]] (n even) and A_1=[[3/2,1/2],[0,1]] (n odd, accelerated map) acting on (n,1)^T; a path-complete family over the parity alphabet is literally an automaton-indexed certificate, and the memory-M De Bruijn nodes = conditioning on the last M parities = residue classes mod 2^M, so their canonical hierarchy has an exact arithmetic meaning for us. GAPS: (i) the theorem is for UNCONSTRAINED products, and the unconstrained JSR of our pair is 3/2 (all-odd word), so it certifies nothing directly; (ii) affine offsets force a unit eigenvalue after homogenization, so gamma<1 is impossible on the lift — only the linear (scalar) part can contract; (iii) their V_i are norms on R^n, ours only need to be defined on the affine line y=1.
finds.transfer: Reinterpret our DFA certificates as {0,infinity}-valued graph Lyapunov functions and relax to finite weights: because the linear part of each branch is the SCALAR 1/2 or 3/2, quadratic templates collapse to one positive scalar weight per automaton state, and the edge inequalities become w_{q'} * a_sigma <= gamma * w_q — an LP whose feasibility is equivalent to max cycle mean of log-weights < log gamma (Karp's algorithm). Concrete statement to prove: for a candidate value-reading automaton Q abstracting the base-2 transduction, feasibility of this LP with gamma<1 implies every orbit whose run stays in Q is ultimately bounded in an explicit ball, hence lands in a computable finite set.

finds.ref: M. Philippe, R. Essick, G. E. Dullerud, R. M. Jungers, "Stability of discrete-time switching systems with constrained switching sequences", Automatica 72:242-250, 2016 (with converse-Lyapunov companion arXiv:1410.7197)
finds.the_theorem: For S(G,Sigma) with G a strongly connected labeled automaton and Sigma a finite matrix set: Prop 2.2: the constrained JSR rho^(S) = inf over multinorms (one norm per automaton node, |A_sigma x|_w <= gamma |x|_v per edge) of their value. Thm 2.3: S stable iff it admits a multinorm with value < 1 (converse Lyapunov). Thm 2.4: an extremal multinorm exists iff |A_p x| <= K rho^^{|p|} |x| for all paths. Thm 3.1/3.2/Cor 3.3: quadratic multinorms are an SDP with guarantee rho^ <= gamma* <= n^{1/(2T)} rho^ under the T-product lift; T = ceil(log n / (2 log(1+r))) gives relative error r. Cor 3.6: M-path-dependent lift gives n^{1/(2(M+1))}.
finds.the_match: This is exactly "weights instead of membership": a multinorm assigns a quadratic weight to each state of the parity/transducer automaton, generalizing our DFA certificates (invariant set = infinite weight outside, unit weight inside). Instantiation: Sigma = {A_0, A_1} (lifted 2x2), G = product of the mod-2^m residue automaton with a candidate base-2 value DFA. What is certified: CJSR<1 gives uniform exponential stability over ALL sequences the automaton accepts — for us, over-approximating the true state-dependent parity law, which is sound for the safety direction. GAPS: (i) linear theory; our affine lift pins rho^ >= 1, so the certificate must target the scalar linear part plus an offset-summability argument, or the marginal boundedness theory (see Philippe-Millerioux-Jungers find); (ii) G must be strongly connected — our certificate DFAs need SCC condensation first; (iii) exactness lost because integer admissibility is state-dependent, the automaton constraint is only an abstraction. PRACTICALITY: n=2 means 3 SDP variables per state; thousands of states trivial; in the scalar collapse it is an LP feasible at millions of states.
finds.transfer: Quantitative generalization of the failed <=7-state search: enumerate (or synthesize by LP duality) automata Q over base-2 transducer states such that (a) Q soundly abstracts T on a regular set X of integers, (b) the multinorm LP over Q is feasible with gamma<1 on linear parts. By their converse theorem the family is COMPLETE relative to the abstraction: if the true constrained growth rate over Q's language is <1, a finite certificate exists at some product-lift level T. Target statement: "every orbit entering regular set X is ultimately bounded, hence eventually periodic in a known finite set" — an upgrade of membership certificates to convergence certificates for regular basins.

finds.ref: B. Legat, R. M. Jungers, P. A. Parrilo, "Certifying Unstability of Switched Systems Using Sum of Squares Programming", SIAM J. Control and Optimization 58(4):2616-2642, 2020
finds.the_theorem: All results stated for automaton-constrained switched systems. Thm 2.4: the value gamma* of the Lyapunov program (max-of-norms over automaton states) EQUALS the CJSR EQUALS the value of the dual program over edge-indexed occupation measures. Cor 2.5: if nontrivial measures mu_{uv,sigma} per automaton edge satisfy sum_in (A_sigma)#mu >= gamma sum_out mu at every node, then gamma <= CJSR — a checkable certificate of growth (instability). Rounding procedures extract explicit switching sequences with guaranteed asymptotic growth; atomic dual solutions correspond to periodic trajectories, recoverable from moments.
finds.the_match: Divergence certificates are instability certificates, and this is the only framework found that certifies the GROWTH direction under automaton constraints. A hypothetical divergent Collatz orbit induces (by weak-* compactness of empirical measures) an invariant occupation measure on any sound abstraction automaton with log-drift >= 0; their dual program searches exactly this space. GAPS, honestly large: a CJSR lower bound only certifies a SYMBOLIC growing sequence, and for Collatz those exist trivially (the all-odd word is realized by a 2-adic integer, not a positive integer), so their certificate can never by itself certify integer divergence; the value is contrapositive — infeasibility of drift->=0 measures on a refined value-reading automaton would certify no divergent orbit with that itinerary structure, and complementary slackness localizes where hypothetical divergence must concentrate.
finds.transfer: Run their primal-dual pair on value-refined abstractions of the Collatz transduction: dual occupation measures with drift >= 0 give a quantitative profile of the residue/digit statistics any divergent orbit must have (a certificate-grade sharpening of density theorems: e.g., certified lower bounds on the frequency of specific parity blocks in a divergent orbit, per automaton). Same machinery applies verbatim to Antihydra/Hydra: occupation measures on the mod-2^k abstraction of n -> floor(3n/2) with counter-drift <= 0 characterize how halting could occur; infeasibility over a refinement = non-halting certificate.

finds.ref: X. Dai, "A Gel'fand-type spectral radius formula and stability of linear constrained switching systems", Linear Algebra and its Applications 436(5):1099-1113, 2012
finds.the_theorem: Thm A: let S: I -> C^{dxd} be continuous (I a metric space) and Lambda a COMPACT SHIFT-INVARIANT subset of the one-sided sequence space Sigma_I^+. Then the constrained generalized spectral radius (limsup_n of sup over admissible length-n words of rho(S_w)^{1/n}, spectral radii of finite products) equals the constrained joint spectral radius (norm growth). Berger-Wang with constraints; Lambda need NOT be sofic/regular — any compact invariant set qualifies. Thm B: equivalences for absolute asymptotic stability over Lambda.
finds.the_match: Take Lambda = closure in {0,1}^N of the set of true parity itineraries of Collatz orbits (shift-invariant since tails of itineraries are itineraries; compact by closure). The theorem applies to the TRUE non-regular constraint language, not just regular abstractions — the only result in this hunt with that strength. Consequence: the maximal exponential growth rate over the closed itinerary system is approached by spectral radii of finite admissible products, i.e., by PERIODIC parity words; periodic parity words are realized exactly by 2-adic rationals with odd denominator = rational cycles of T. GAP: the closure operation adds limit words not realized by any integer, and the sup may be attained only at such points; so this transfers growth questions to rational-cycle spectra but cannot distinguish integer from 2-adic realization.
finds.transfer: Statement to prove/compute: the extremal growth rate of the Collatz symbolic system restricted to (closure of) integer itineraries equals sup over rational T-cycles of (3^{odd}/2^{len})^{1/len}, linking divergence-rate bounds to the well-studied rational cycle structure (Boehm-Sontacchi style bounds become certificate-grade bounds on the constrained generalized spectral radius). Compute: enumerate admissible cycles of value-refined automata and their products' spectral radii to get certified two-sided bounds on the growth rate of any hypothetical divergent orbit class.

finds.ref: V. Kozyakin, "Matrix products with constraints on the sliding block relative frequencies of different factors", Linear Algebra Appl. 457:244-260, 2014; and "The Berger-Wang formula for the Markovian joint spectral radius", Linear Algebra Appl. 448:315-328, 2014
finds.the_theorem: Thm 1 (1403.5050): the set of infinite sequences over a finite alphabet whose EVERY sliding window of length l has symbol frequencies within prescribed bounds [p^-, p^+] is an l-step topological Markov chain (subshift of finite type), hence representable by an explicit finite automaton. Thm 2: for matrix products indexed by such frequency-constrained sequences, the frequency-constrained generalized spectral radius equals the frequency-constrained JSR (Berger-Wang analog; builds on Dai's Markovian case).
finds.the_match: The Collatz drift argument is a frequency statement: a product along a word with odd-frequency p has linear-part growth (3/2)^p (1/2)^{1-p}, which is <1 iff p < log2/log3 = 0.6309...; divergent orbits must have limsup odd-density >= log2/log3. Kozyakin's SFT construction converts "odd-frequency <= p in every window of length l" into an explicit automaton on which the multinorm/max-cycle-mean certificates of the other finds run mechanically. GAPS: sliding-window frequency bounds are strictly stronger than asymptotic density bounds (a divergent orbit could have low-density windows infinitely often), so the certified region misses orbits with irregular parity statistics; and the constraint is symbolic — no integer-realizability input.
finds.transfer: Certified finitary version of the folklore density lemma: for every p < log2/log3 and window length l, an explicit LP certificate proving "every Collatz orbit whose itinerary keeps odd-frequency <= p in all l-windows is ultimately bounded, with explicit entry time and ball radius". Contrapositive with explicit constants: a divergent orbit must contain, for every l, infinitely many l-windows of odd-density > log2/log3 - epsilon(l). Same construction for Antihydra with the counter-drift weights (+2 even, -1 odd): halting requires window-densities crossing the drift threshold 1/3 against measure.

finds.ref: M. Philippe, G. Millerioux, R. M. Jungers, "Deciding the boundedness and dead-beat stability of constrained switching systems", Nonlinear Analysis: Hybrid Systems 23:100-115, 2017 (arXiv:1512.04887)
finds.the_theorem: For automaton-constrained switched linear systems S(G,M) with constrained growth rate rho^(S)=1: a DECIDABLE sufficient condition for uniform boundedness of all constrained products ("nodal irreducibility": at every node v, for every nontrivial subspace X there is a cycle c at v with A_c X not contained in X), generalizing the classical irreducibility=>boundedness result. Also a polynomial-time algorithm deciding rho^(S)=0 (dead-beat). They note boundedness at rho^=1 is undecidable in general (citing Blondel-Tsitsiklis).
finds.the_match: Our homogenized affine system sits EXACTLY at their marginal case: every product A_w = [[lambda_w, c_w],[0,1]] has eigenvalue 1, so rho^ >= 1 always, and "no divergent orbit with itinerary in L" = boundedness of the constrained products at rho^ = 1 (when lambda contracts, products converge to [[0,c_inf],[0,1]] — bounded, not stable). GAP, structural: our pair shares the invariant subspace span{(1,0)} (upper triangular), so nodal irreducibility FAILS for us and their sufficient condition is inapplicable as stated; however the reducibility is benign — boundedness reduces to convergence of the weighted series c_w = sum of offsets weighted by products of scalars, decidable by the max-cycle-mean of the scalar weights over the automaton. Their framework poses the right decision problem; our triangular specialization needs (and plausibly admits) a bespoke complete decision procedure.
finds.transfer: Prove the specialization: for triangular pairs with scalar linear parts, boundedness of automaton-constrained products at rho^=1 is DECIDABLE (max cycle mean of log-scalar weights < 0 on every reachable SCC iff bounded, with explicit bound max|c|/(1-gamma)). This yields an ALGORITHM that takes any candidate regular itinerary language L and decides whether L is quantitatively divergence-free — replacing per-certificate ad hoc arguments in our DFA search and extending it from membership to convergence certificates.

finds.ref: V. D. Blondel, J. N. Tsitsiklis, "The boundedness of all products of a pair of matrices is undecidable", Systems & Control Letters 41(2):135-140, 2000
finds.the_theorem: Input: a finite set Sigma of n x n RATIONAL matrices (n part of the input). Thm 2: deciding whether the semigroup of all products is bounded is undecidable, and deciding whether JSR(Sigma) <= 1 is undecidable, EVEN when Sigma has exactly two matrices. Proof: reduction from emptiness of probabilistic finite automata (Thm 1); the construction produces pairs whose dimension grows with the PFA. Consequences: extremal-norm existence and marginal boundedness questions undecidable in general.
finds.the_match: Divergence certificates ask (after homogenization) whether an automaton-constrained sub-semigroup generated by a FIXED pair of 2x2 upper-triangular rational matrices is bounded on integer vectors — an instance of their undecidable problem family, but far inside special hypotheses: dimension fixed at 2, triangular, scalar linear parts, constrained products, single initial vector. Their theorem says NOTHING about this instance (undecidability at fixed dimension 2 is not known; most 2x2 semigroup problems trend decidable — see Potapov-Semukhin find). The honest reading: hardness of Collatz cannot be blamed on matrix-product undecidability; it must enter through the state-dependent (arithmetic) admissibility, i.e., the interaction of the language constraint with integrality.
finds.transfer: Boundary-mapping statement to prove or refute: "boundedness of automaton-constrained products of a fixed pair of 2x2 upper-triangular rational matrices on a rational vector is decidable" (conjecturally yes via the scalar collapse; contrast with their n-part-of-input undecidability). A positive proof would formally certify that ALL quantitative certificate checks in our program are algorithmic, and would sharpen where Conway-style undecidability of generalized Collatz families actually enters: in the transduction (state-dependent switching), not the matrix products.

finds.ref: G. Aazan, A. Girard, L. Greco, P. Mason, "A Joint Spectral Radius for omega-Regular Language-Driven Switched Linear Systems", in Hybrid and Networked Dynamical Systems, LNCIS vol. 493, Springer, 2024 (earlier version HSCC 2022)
finds.the_theorem: For switched linear systems whose switching signals form an omega-regular language given by a DETERMINISTIC Buchi automaton: define the omega-regular JSR (omega-RJSR) measuring the worst-case contraction of the state between successive visits to ACCEPTING states along accepted runs. Theorems: omega-RJSR < 1 characterizes convergence to zero of trajectories along all signals in the language; Lyapunov-like characterization with automaton-state-indexed norms whose contraction is enforced only on first-return words to the accepting set; relations to the classical constrained JSR and computational procedures via lifted automata.
finds.the_match: Collatz-side conditions are natively omega-regular: any divergent orbit's itinerary satisfies the Buchi condition "odd infinitely often" (and much more), and our DFA divergence certificates are safety/co-Buchi objects on infinite runs (avoid the word of 1 forever). The amortization is the key structural fit: individual odd steps EXPAND (3/2), so any certificate for Collatz must allow transient growth and demand contraction only per return to a checkpoint set — exactly the omega-RJSR design, unavailable in plain multinorm frameworks. GAPS: deterministic Buchi automata cannot express all omega-regular languages (though our safety/parity conditions fit); the theory is linear (same affine/marginal caveat as everywhere); and their contraction-per-return still only quantifies over symbolic runs, not integer-realizable ones.
finds.transfer: Set checkpoints = accepting states at "value returns below its running minimum scale" abstracted in a digit automaton; statement to prove: existence of state-indexed weights contracting on every first-return admissible word certifies "every orbit with itinerary accepted by the Buchi abstraction visits the checkpoint infinitely often with geometrically decreasing scale", i.e., convergence — a formal home for amortized descent arguments (the same shape as Terras/Tao-style stopping-time arguments) as searchable certificates. For Space Needle: the accepting condition "value + v2-valuation hits a power of 2" is a reachability/Buchi functional of the same run space.

finds.ref: I. Potapov, P. Semukhin, "Decidability of the membership problem for 2x2 integer matrices", SODA 2017, pp. 170-186; and V. Diekert, I. Potapov, P. Semukhin, "Decidability of membership problems for flat rational subsets of GL(2,Q) and singular matrices", ISSAC 2020 / SIAM J. Computing (arXiv:1910.02302v6, 2024)
finds.the_theorem: (i) Membership in finitely generated subsemigroups of nonsingular 2x2 INTEGER matrices is decidable (translation of matrix products to word combinatorics in GL(2,Z) plus regular-language emptiness). (ii) Emptiness of Boolean combinations of flat rational subsets FRat(GL(2,Q), GL(2,Z)) (finite unions g_0 L_1 g_1 ... L_t g_t, L_i rational over GL(2,Z)) is decidable. (iii) Dichotomy: a finitely generated group G with GL(2,Z) < G < GL(2,Q) is either isomorphic to GL(2,Z) x Z^k (decidable rational-subset membership) or contains an infinite-index extension of a Baumslag-Solitar group BS(1,q), q>=2, where rational-subset membership is OPEN.
finds.the_match: Sharpest instantiation found. Because both branch maps strictly decrease 2-adic valuation once it is negative, an endpoint-integral product forces pathwise integrality, i.e., parity-consistency is SELF-ENFORCING: "the T-orbit of n reaches 1" is equivalent to plain vector reachability (1,1)^T in {A_0,A_1}* (n,1)^T in the FREE 2-generator subsemigroup of GL(2,Q) — no language constraint at all. The Collatz conjecture is a uniform vector reachability statement in a f.g. subsemigroup of GL(2,Q) whose generators (dets 1/2, 3/4; entries in Z[1/6]) generate exactly a Baumslag-Solitar-type affine group — the open cell of their dichotomy, strictly outside both decidable classes (integer matrices; GL(2,Z)-flat sets). GAPS: membership and vector reachability are different problems (they have separate SL(2,Z) vector-reachability results); their decidability techniques (regular languages over GL(2,Z) generators) do not directly handle denominators 2 and 3 simultaneously; nothing here certifies divergence or convergence — this find is a placement result.
finds.transfer: Two concrete moves: (1) prove decidability of vector reachability for the SPECIFIC pair {A_0, A_1} with target (1,1) restricted to integer source vectors, by importing their words-to-regular-languages machinery localized at the primes 2 and 3 (Bruhat-Tits tree actions of GL(2,Q_2) and GL(2,Q_3): the semigroup acts on the product of two trees, and our base-2 DFA certificates are shadows of the 2-tree action — a principled route to richer-than-regular certificates); (2) formalize "Collatz sits in the BS(1,q) open cell" as a statement delimiting which certificate families can possibly be complete.

best_single_bet: Play out the Philippe-Essick-Dullerud-Jungers constrained-JSR multinorm framework (find 2) fused with the scalar collapse: because Collatz's linear parts are scalars (1/2, 3/2), the entire path-complete/multinorm certificate family over ANY finite abstraction automaton collapses to per-state scalar weights, and feasibility is exactly 'max cycle mean of log-weights < 0' — an LP/Karp computation feasible at millions of states, versus the 7-state exhaustion of the membership search. The converse-Lyapunov theorems (finds 2 and 4) make this family COMPLETE relative to the abstraction, and we proved a no-go along the way: any abstraction carrying only bounded residue information (mod 2^m) admits every parity word (Terras), has constrained JSR 3/2, and can never certify anything — so weights must be indexed by states of value-reading (base-2 transducer) automata, i.e., precisely the quantitative upgrade of our existing DFA certificates from membership to convergence ('every orbit entering regular set X is ultimately bounded, hence eventually periodic in a computable finite set'). FIRST MOVE (one afternoon): take the existing <=7-state DFA enumeration harness, replace the exact-invariance check with Karp max-cycle-mean over the product automaton with edge weights log(3/2) on odd branches and log(1/2) on even branches, and rerun the enumeration — every automaton that failed as an exact invariant set but has all reachable cycles below odd-density log2/log3 becomes a valid quantitative certificate; then scale the search to thousands of states via LP duality instead of enumeration, and use Legat-Jungers-Parrilo dual occupation measures (find 3) on the infeasible automata to read off exactly which cycle statistics block certification, guiding the next refinement.
dead_ends: (1) Path-complete certificates over pure parity or mod-2^m residue abstractions: provably useless — all 2^k finite parity words are integer-admissible (Terras), so every sound residue-only abstraction accepts the full shift and its constrained JSR equals the unconstrained 3/2 > 1; no certificate of any template can be feasible. Do not re-search 'path-complete Lyapunov for Collatz-like parity systems' — the abstraction, not the template, is the bottleneck. (2) State-dependent-switching stability literature (Della Rossa-Jungers graph-based feedback stabilization, min-switching/Lyapunov-Metzler for switched affine systems): it DESIGNS the switching signal (controller picks the mode), while our mode is dictated by arithmetic — the transferable content is already contained in the constrained-JSR abstraction view; nothing new there. (3) Dedicated path-complete theory for switched AFFINE systems: does not exist in usable form; practical-stability results for affine switched systems are controller-oriented; homogenization plus the marginal rho=1 boundedness theory (find 6) is the right substitute. (4) Blondel-Tsitsiklis undecidability as an explanation of Collatz-certificate hardness: does not bite — their constructions need dimension as part of the input; at fixed 2x2 the trend is decidability (Potapov-Semukhin), so hardness must enter via arithmetic admissibility, not matrix products. (5) The 'Approximation of the constrained JSR via algebraic lifting' paper (arXiv:1807.03965, downloaded) adds only implementation detail beyond finds 1-2 — no new certificate power. (6) Springer paywalled page for the omega-regular JSR chapter — use the open HAL version (hal-04527099).
